text:
ONCOLOGY CLINIC FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Margaret Okonkwo
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for disease reassessment and review of restaging imaging. Patient with stage IV clear cell RCC, now approximately 3 months into second-line cabozantinib following progression on first-line nivolumab/ipilimumab.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with a history of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, diagnosed approximately 12 months ago. Her disease at presentation was characterized by a large right renal primary (11.4 cm, pT3a) with retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (5/8 nodes positive at nephrectomy, the largest with extranodal extension) and bilateral pulmonary metastases — IMDC intermediate risk, 2 factors. NGS on the primary tumor revealed a genomically complex tumor: biallelic VHL loss, PBRM1 frameshift, BAP1 nonsense mutation, SETD2 frameshift, TP53 R248W, with chromosome 3p deletion, MYC gain, and CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion. PD-L1 CPS 18 on surgical specimen.

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy followed by first-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg q3 weeks x 4 induction cycles, then maintenance nivo 480 mg q4 weeks. She achieved a partial response in her dominant pulmonary lesions on the first restaging scan (~4 months into therapy), though with some lesion stability elsewhere. Course was complicated by grade 2 immune colitis (cycle 2, managed with prednisone taper and one held ipi dose) and grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (~5 months on therapy, now on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily). Unfortunately, restaging at approximately 9 months demonstrated unequivocal disease progression with enlargement of all pulmonary nodules, two new right middle lobe nodules, and two new right hepatic lobe lesions (0.8 and 0.6 cm), prompting discontinuation of nivolumab and transition to cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg PO daily.

Cabozantinib was started approximately 3 months ago. Early course was complicated by grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome, HFS) and grade 2 diarrhea, both requiring a dose reduction to 40 mg daily, as well as grade 2 hypertension managed by uptitrating amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg. At the 6-week check, toxicities were improving: HFS downgraded to grade 1, diarrhea controlled at grade 1, and BP normalized.

She is here today to review the results of her restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (obtained earlier this week). She reports overall feeling reasonably well. Fatigue remains a persistent background symptom. She denies new cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, dyspnea, or pleuritic symptoms. No new abdominal pain. Appetite is reduced compared to baseline but she has been able to maintain oral intake. Bowel habits are approximately 2 loose stools per day above her prior baseline (grade 1); she uses loperamide occasionally. Feet remain tender but she has adapted with appropriate footwear and urea cream. Blood pressure has been in the 130s/80s range at home monitoring per patient report.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: moderate fatigue (chronic, baseline on TKI), grade 1 loose stools (2/day above baseline), mild bilateral foot and hand tenderness and skin dryness, mild cold intolerance (thyroid related, stable), mild decreased appetite, weight loss (~4 kg total since starting cabo — stable over the past 4-6 weeks by patient report).
Negative: no hemoptysis, no new cough or dyspnea, no chest pain, no nausea or vomiting, no hematuria, no fever or chills, no bone pain, no new neurological symptoms, no rash (other than chronic HFS changes), no oral mucositis, no bleeding.

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg PO daily
2. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (immune-related hypothyroidism, on nivolumab)
3. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily (hypertension, pre-existing and cabozantinib-exacerbated)
4. Loperamide 2 mg PO PRN (up to 4 doses/day for diarrhea)
5. Urea 40% cream topical BID to palms and soles

Allergies: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing woman, alert and oriented x3, no acute distress. Slightly fatigued affect but interactive and engaged.
Vitals: BP 134/82 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.0°C, Weight: [recorded by nursing staff].
HEENT: No oral mucositis, no thrush. Thyroid non-tender, no goiter palpable.
Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary adenopathy.
Chest/Cardiovascular: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Well-healed right flank nephrectomy scar. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No right upper quadrant tenderness. No palpable masses.
Extremities: Bilateral palms with mild residual dry, hyperkeratotic skin changes — no active blistering or open wounds. Bilateral plantar surfaces with mild callus formation and peeling at weight-bearing areas. No grade 2 or higher HFS features today.
Neurological: Grossly intact. No focal deficits.
Skin: No rash elsewhere. No jaundice.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today)
CBC: WBC 6.1 K/uL, Hgb 10.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable from 10.8 at prior visit), Plt 198 K/uL.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138, K 3.9, Cl 101, CO2 25, BUN 22, Cr 1.4 mg/dL (stable, s/p nephrectomy), eGFR ~48 mL/min/1.73m2. Glucose 92. Total bilirubin 0.7, ALT 28, AST 24, Alk phos 88 (all within normal limits). Total protein and albumin normal.
Thyroid function: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine — stable), free T4 1.0 ng/dL (normal).
Lipase: normal.
Urinalysis: trace proteinuria (1+), no hematuria — to monitor; cabozantinib can cause proteinuria.
Coagulation: not obtained today.

---

IMAGING RESULTS
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (most recent, reviewed today):

Chest: The dominant right lower lobe lesion is slightly smaller, measuring approximately 1.9 cm (down from 2.4 cm at start of cabozantinib). Left upper lobe nodule has shrunk modestly to 0.9 cm (from 1.2 cm). Right upper lobe nodule at 1.1 cm (from 1.4 cm). Left lower lobe at 0.9 cm (from 1.1 cm). The two right middle lobe nodules identified at progression are small and stable in the 0.4-0.5 cm range. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion.

Abdomen/pelvis: The two right hepatic lobe lesions are stable — 0.8 and 0.6 cm. No new hepatic lesions. Nephrectomy bed clean, no local recurrence. No new retroperitoneal or pelvic adenopathy. No ascites. Adrenal glands unremarkable.

Bones: No new osseous lesions.

Overall: Compared to prior imaging at the time of starting cabozantinib, there has been minor shrinkage in the larger pulmonary lesions and stability of the hepatic deposits. No new sites of disease. Clinical impression: disease is under control on current therapy — meaningful improvement in dominant lung disease without frank progression.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IV CLEAR CELL RCC WITH SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION — STABLE DISEASE ON SECOND-LINE CABOZANTINIB
Restaging CT today is reassuring. We see minor but real reductions in the dominant pulmonary lesions and stability of the hepatic disease, with no new sites of involvement. While the overall picture does not cross a threshold for formal partial response, this is meaningful clinical benefit — particularly given the aggressive genomic profile (BAP1 loss, TP53 R248W, sarcomatoid differentiation), which historically predicts a more compressed disease course. The patient has now derived approximately 3 months of disease control on cabozantinib after progressing through ipi/nivo. We will continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Discussed with patient — she expressed relief and agreed to continue on current therapy. Plan for next restaging CT in 3 months.

2. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (PALMAR-PLANTAR ERYTHRODYSESTHESIA) — GRADE 1, IMPROVING
Currently at grade 1, well-managed. Patient is adherent with urea 40% cream BID, appropriate footwear, and avoidance of high-friction activities. No dose change needed at this time. Will continue current supportive measures. Dermatology follow-up at patient's discretion.

3. DIARRHEA — GRADE 1, CONTROLLED
Approximately 1-2 loose stools above baseline daily, manageable with dietary modification and PRN loperamide. Patient is appropriately hydrated. No dose modification warranted. Reinforced BRAT elements and low-residue diet when symptomatic.

4. HYPERTENSION — WELL CONTROLLED
BP 134/82 in clinic today. Controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. No further uptitration needed currently. Will continue monitoring at each visit. If BP trends up again, will discuss adding an ACE inhibitor or ARB (noting renal function will need monitoring given single kidney and existing mild CKD).

5. HYPOTHYROIDISM (IMMUNE-RELATED, FROM PRIOR NIVOLUMAB) — STABLE
TSH 2.8, free T4 normal on levothyroxine 75 mcg. Patient has no significant hypothyroid symptoms. Continue current dose; recheck TFTs at next scheduled visit.

6. MILD ANEMIA — MONITORING
Hgb 10.6 g/dL, down marginally from 10.8. Likely multifactorial — chronic disease, prior nephrectomy, TKI effect. No transfusion indicated currently. Iron studies not recently checked — will order today to rule out iron deficiency component (dietary intake may be suboptimal). No plans for ESA at this time.

7. TRACE PROTEINURIA — MONITOR
New 1+ proteinuria on UA today, likely cabozantinib-related (VEGFR inhibition class effect). Will obtain spot urine protein:creatinine ratio at next visit. If nephrotic-range, would discuss nephrology referral and dose adjustment.

8. WEIGHT LOSS / NUTRITION — MONITORING
Total ~4 kg since starting TKI. Patient reports appetite slightly reduced but stable over past month. No significant nausea, dysphagia, or early satiety. Will ensure nutritionist referral is active. If further decline, will revisit dose modification or caloric supplementation.

9. GOALS OF CARE / PSYCHOSOCIAL
Social work referral was placed at last visit. Patient confirmed she has spoken with social work x1. Exploring community resources. Patient continues to express desire for active treatment. Palliative care consultation was gently reintroduced today — she remains hesitant but is open to a future conversation. Emphasized that palliative care is supportive and complementary to active treatment, not a transition away from it.

Return to clinic in approximately 6 weeks for toxicity check. Restaging CT in 3 months. Labs including iron studies, spot urine protein:creatinine, and TFTs to be ordered today.

Time spent with patient: ~40 minutes, greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
